era_context: Late 14th century: Ming Dynasty consolidates power in China; Ottoman Empire expands in the Balkans; Western Christendom fractured by the Great Schism (1378–1417); Timurid Renaissance in Persia; Joseon Dynasty in Korea; Aztec Empire in Mesoamerica.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe and Scandinavia (sparse specific records for this year)

events.title: Peasants' Revolt in England
events.description: The Peasants' Revolt erupted in England in June 1381, sparked by the Poll Tax and broader grievances over serfdom and labor conditions. Led by figures such as Wat Tyler and John Ball, the revolt saw peasants and urban workers march on London, storm the Tower of London, and demand the abolition of serfdom. King Richard II, then only 14 years old, met with the rebels and promised concessions. However, the revolt was brutally suppressed within weeks, with Wat Tyler killed and thousands of rebels executed. The revolt failed to achieve its immediate goals but exposed deep social tensions and contributed to long-term changes in labor relations and peasant consciousness.

sources.name: Knighton's Chronicle (Henry Knighton, contemporary English chronicler)
Walsingham's Historia Anglicana (Thomas Walsingham, contemporary chronicler)

events.certainty_note: The Peasants' Revolt is one of the best-documented events of the 14th century, with multiple contemporary chronicles and official records providing consistent accounts of the major events and dates.
cross_references: Part of broader late medieval social unrest

events.title: Battle of Kulikovo Field (alternative dating)
events.description: Some sources date the Battle of Kulikovo Field to 1381, though 1380 is more commonly accepted. In this battle, Dmitri Donskoi, Grand Prince of Moscow, led a coalition of Russian principalities against the Golden Horde forces under Mamai. The Russian victory was symbolically significant, demonstrating that Mongol-Tatar forces could be defeated, though Mongol overlordship would persist for another century. The battle became a foundational myth in Russian national consciousness.
events.certainty_note: The Battle of Kulikovo Field is traditionally dated to 1380, but some sources place it in 1381. The discrepancy reflects ambiguity in medieval chronicle dating. 1380 is more widely accepted by modern scholars.

events.title: Portuguese maritime expansion: Diogo Cão's continued voyages
events.description: Portuguese explorer Diogo Cão continued his voyages along the West African coast, establishing trading posts and erecting stone pillars (padrões) to mark Portuguese claims. These expeditions, sponsored by Prince Henry the Navigator, were part of the systematic Portuguese exploration of the African coast that would eventually lead to the rounding of the Cape of Good Hope and the opening of the sea route to Asia.
events.certainty_note: Diogo Cão's voyages are well-documented, though the precise chronology of individual expeditions varies slightly between sources.
cross_references: Part of broader Portuguese Age of Exploration (1415–1600s)

events.title: Ming Dynasty consolidation: Zhu Yuanzhang's purges
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor (Zhu Yuanzhang) continued his consolidation of power through administrative reforms and purges of potential rivals. In 1381, he abolished the position of Prime Minister (Chancellor), concentrating power directly in his hands. This move was part of a broader pattern of centralization that would characterize Ming governance. The purges and administrative changes eliminated potential sources of opposition but also created a more autocratic system.
events.certainty_note: The abolition of the Prime Minister position in 1381 is well-documented in Ming official records.

disconfirming_evidence: The Peasants' Revolt is sometimes romanticized as a proto-democratic uprising, but modern scholarship emphasizes its limited social base, its focus on specific grievances (particularly the Poll Tax), and the rapid restoration of feudal order. The revolt did not fundamentally alter the feudal system, though it did contribute to long-term changes in labor relations. The Battle of Kulikovo Field is traditionally dated to 1380, not 1381; the 1381 dating appears in some sources but is less widely accepted. The significance of the battle was partly mythologized in later Russian historiography, which emphasized Moscow's eventual triumph over the Golden Horde.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1381 is moderate to good. English chronicles provide exceptionally detailed accounts of the Peasants' Revolt, with multiple contemporary sources. Russian, Chinese, and Portuguese sources provide reasonable coverage of other major events. The year is well-documented in Western Europe but less so in other regions. African, American, and Oceanic histories remain severely underrepresented in surviving written records. The density of documentation is uneven across regions, with Western Europe and East Asia better represented than other areas.

graph_edges.note: The Peasants' Revolt of 1381 was followed by legal consolidation and repression in 1382.
Zhu Yuanzhang's consolidation of power in 1381 continued until his death in 1398.
